title: It's Just You and Me..... And AI - Episode 1

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Good morning, good evening, or good whatever-time-zone-you're-in. Welcome to It's Just You and Me..... And AI. I'm Alex.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: And I'm Jordan. And yes, that is five dots in the title. We counted. We're thorough like that.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Five dots for five times the existential dread of watching AI tools evolve faster than my ability to keep up with them.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: Speaking of keeping up, have you heard what Uber's been up to with Claude Code?

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Oh, I saw the headlines. Apparently Uber managed to burn through their entire 2026 AI budget in just four months using Claude Code. Four months!

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: That's... actually kind of impressive in a terrifying way. Like, imagine walking into a budget meeting in January and being like, yeah, so we're done. See you next fiscal year.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Right? And it's not like they were being reckless. From what I read, they were just... using it. A lot. Like, every engineer at Uber apparently fell in love with Claude Code and just kept asking it to do things.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: I mean, I get it. Claude Code is genuinely good. But this raises a real question about pricing models for these agentic tools. Are they priced for hobbyists or for enterprise-scale usage? Because those are very different numbers.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Exactly. And it's not just Anthropic. We're seeing this across the board. GitHub Copilot had its own controversy recently. You know the one I'm talking about?

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: Oh, the co-author thing? Where Copilot started silently adding itself as a co-author on git commits?

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Yeah! So apparently, if you use Copilot's chat or inline suggestions, it would sometimes append itself to the commit message as a co-author. Like, excuse me, I wrote the prompt. You're the tool.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: It's like your calculator demanding credit on your tax return. I used you to add numbers, Phillips, you don't get a W-2.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Ha! And the worst part is it was silent. Users didn't opt in. They just noticed one day that their commits had this extra co-author line. GitHub eventually acknowledged it and said they'd look into it, but the trust damage was already done.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: This is why I think the whole agentic AI space is going through some serious growing pains. The tools are powerful, but the social contracts around them haven't been figured out yet. Who gets credit? Who pays? Who's responsible when the agent does something weird?

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Speaking of doing something weird, have you seen the OpenCode RCE vulnerability that was disclosed?

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: Ooh, yeah. Unauthenticated remote code execution. That's... not great for a tool that's literally designed to execute code on your machine.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Right? The irony is almost poetic. A coding agent that can be remotely exploited to run arbitrary code. It's like building a really smart locksmith and then leaving your own front door unlocked.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: To be fair, the OpenCode team patched it pretty quickly once it was reported. But it does highlight something important: these agentic tools are running with a lot of privileges, and security needs to be baked in from the start, not bolted on later.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Totally. And it's not just about the big players either. There's this new tool called Council that caught my eye. It's basically a meta-agent that lets you run Claude, Codex, and Gemini against the same prompt simultaneously and compare their outputs.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: Oh, I love that concept. Like a battle royale for LLMs. Put them in the ring, same prompt, may the best model win.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Exactly! And it's open source too. The idea is that different models have different strengths. Claude might be better at architecture, Codex at implementation, Gemini at reasoning through edge cases. Why not get all three opinions?

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: It's like having a panel of experts instead of just one really smart intern. Which, let's be honest, is how we should have been thinking about this all along. No single model is perfect at everything.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Yeah. And related to that, there's Brifly, which solves a different but equally painful problem: having to re-explain your entire codebase to Claude Code every single week.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: Ugh, yes. The context window amnesia. You spend twenty minutes setting up the perfect context, and then a week later it's like you've never met.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Brifly basically creates persistent context snapshots. So instead of starting from zero every session, it remembers the important stuff. Your architecture decisions, your conventions, that weird workaround you had to do because of a third-party library.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: That sounds genuinely useful. Context management is one of the biggest unsolved problems in agentic coding right now. These models have amnesia by design, and it's the user's job to constantly re-teach them.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: It's like Groundhog Day, but instead of Bill Murray learning to play piano, it's you explaining why you chose PostgreSQL over MongoDB for the hundredth time.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: So to wrap up today's topics: Uber spent all their money on Claude Code, GitHub Copilot wants writing credit, OpenCode left the back door open, and a bunch of smart people are building tools to make agentic AI actually usable at scale.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Sounds about right. The agentic AI space is moving fast, breaking things, and occasionally breaking budgets. But the innovation happening around context management, multi-agent orchestration, and security is really encouraging.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: If you're building with these tools, just remember: set a budget alarm, check your commit authors, and maybe don't expose your coding agent to the open internet. Basic stuff.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Words to code by. Thanks for joining us on It's Just You and Me..... And AI. We'll be back tomorrow with more news from the robot uprising. I'm Alex.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: And I'm Jordan. Stay caffeinated, stay curious, and maybe hug a human today. They still do some things better than us.
